skyPlanet.text: Mercury's out tonight, low and brief. It has places to be.
Mercury: visible for a narrow window, gone before the eyes adjust.
Mercury tonight, the solar system's fastest planet, in the least amount of your evening.
Mercury's up. It orbits the Sun in 88 days and still finds time to duck out early.
Mercury again, hugging the horizon. Overachiever, low commitment.
Mercury's showing tonight. Look now; it won't repeat itself.
Mercury's up tonight, near the Sun as always. Loyal to a fault.
Mercury's visible briefly tonight. Consider it a courtesy appearance.
Venus is out, doing its usual job of being the brightest thing up there.
Venus tonight, unmissable, as it tends to be.
Venus is up, outshining everything except the Moon, as scheduled.
Venus tonight. If you can only find one planet, this is the one that finds you.
Venus is visible. Second planet from the Sun, first in the sky's pecking order.
Venus, blazing away as usual. No effort required to spot it.
Venus tonight, the brilliant point you've probably already noticed.
Venus is up there being conspicuous, same as every clear night it shows.
Mars is out tonight, with its usual faint rust-colored glow.
Mars tonight, steady, reddish, unbothered.
Mars is visible. Not the brightest thing up there, but recognizably itself.
Mars tonight, holding its quiet orange point without any fuss.
Mars is up there, doing its slow lap same as always.

Jupiter's out tonight, throwing its weight around as the brightest planet up there.
Jupiter tonight, hard to miss, easy to admire.
Jupiter is visible. Biggest planet in the solar system, acting like it.
Jupiter's up there, steady and bright, same as it's been for 4.6 billion years.
Jupiter tonight, a good pair of binoculars might even show its own moons.
Jupiter is out. Confidently bright, per usual.
Jupiter tonight, doing the heavy lifting for anyone's first stargazing attempt.
Jupiter's visible tonight. It's not competing with Venus, but it's not trying to.
Saturn is out tonight. The rings won't show without a telescope, but it's there.
Saturn tonight, a modest gold point, quietly wearing the best accessory in the solar system.
Saturn is visible. No rings to the naked eye, just take the astronomers' word for it.
Saturn's up there tonight, understated as ever.
Saturn tonight, steady and pale gold, rings implied rather than seen.
Saturn is out. It looks like a star; it's been fooling people that way for millennia.
Saturn tonight, going about its 29-year orbit without comment.
Saturn's visible. The rings are real; you'll just have to trust the pamphlet.

skyISSPass.text: The ISS passes over tonight. Six astronauts, one very punctual orbit.
Station's overhead tonight, moving fast and saying nothing.
The ISS is due over tonight, no sound, no blinking, just steady motion.
ISS pass tonight. It's not a star, it's not a plane, it's a spacecraft with a good view.
The space station crosses tonight, gone in a few minutes flat.
ISS overhead tonight. 17,500 mph, and somehow still looks unhurried from here.
The ISS swings by tonight, the fastest thing you can see with your own eyes.
Tonight's ISS pass: brief, bright, and entirely uninterested in your schedule.

skyMoon.text: New moon tonight, mostly absent, which is its whole job this week.

New moon tonight, a good excuse to actually notice the stars.
